channel.title: Quantum Crier
channel.description: Daily curated quantum computing news and insights by Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quanscient and Haiqu Demonstrate 15-Step Nonlinear Fluid Simulation on IBM Quantum Hardware
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough represents a significant step toward practical quantum computational fluid dynamics, as simulating nonlinear fluid flows around obstacles is a cornerstone challenge in engineering applications from aerospace design to climate modeling. The successful execution of a 15-step simulation on current IBM quantum hardware suggests that quantum algorithms for complex fluid dynamics are becoming more viable despite the limitations of today's noisy intermediate-scale quantum devices. The collaboration between Quanscient's simulation expertise and Haiqu's middleware optimization highlights how specialized quantum software stacks are becoming crucial for translating theoretical quantum advantages into real engineering workflows. While still early-stage, this work points toward quantum computing eventually tackling the computationally intensive fluid simulations that currently require massive supercomputing resources.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Researchers from Quanscient, a leader in cloud-based multiphysics simulation technology and quantum algorithms, and Haiqu, a leading developer of quantum middleware, today announced a new algorithm that can significantly advance the use of quantum computing in real-world engineering applications. The teams conducted a 15-step nonlinear fluid benchmark with an obstacle, […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: QuEra Launches Open-Source Package to Simulate Logical Quantum Circuits at Scale
item.description:
Curator's Take: This release marks a significant step forward in making quantum error correction research more accessible and practical, as simulating non-Clifford operations like T-gates has traditionally been computationally expensive and limited researchers to small circuit sizes. QuEra's GPU-accelerated approach could dramatically speed up the development cycle for fault-tolerant quantum algorithms by allowing researchers to test logical quantum circuits at scales that were previously prohibitive. The timing is particularly valuable as the quantum computing field increasingly shifts focus toward error correction implementations, with companies like IBM and Google racing to demonstrate practical quantum advantage through fault-tolerant systems. By open-sourcing this tool, QuEra is essentially democratizing access to crucial simulation capabilities that could accelerate the entire field's progress toward scalable, error-corrected quantum computing.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — QuEra Computing today open-sourced its T-gate simulator (Tsim), a GPU-accelerated quantum circuit simulator that, for the first time, lets researchers simulate non-Clifford gate operations at the speed and scale that quantum error correction (QEC) development demands. QEC is the essential bridge between today’s noisy quantum processors and the fault-tolerant machines […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum entanglement between electrons and ions captured at attosecond timescale
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article represents a significant advance in our ability to observe quantum mechanics in real-time, capturing the elusive moment when quantum entanglement forms between different types of particles at the attosecond scale - that's billionths of a billionth of a second. The ability to directly measure entanglement formation between electrons and ions at these ultrafast timescales opens new possibilities for understanding how quantum correlations emerge in natural systems and could inform the development of more robust quantum technologies. While quantum entanglement is the foundation of quantum computing and quantum communication, actually observing it happen in real-time has been incredibly challenging due to the fragile nature of quantum states and the lightning-fast speeds involved. This breakthrough provides researchers with an unprecedented window into the fundamental processes that make quantum technologies possible, potentially leading to better control over entanglement generation and more efficient quantum devices.

Quantum mechanics is extremely successful at describing the behavior of matter at the atomic level. This success forces one to accept that certain aspects of physical reality go far beyond our intuition. Among these, none is more intriguing than the concept of quantum entanglement, which mathematically describes how two particles that have at some point in the past interacted with each other retain a memory of this interaction to such an extent that acting on one of the two particles has a measurable influence on the properties of the other particle, even if the two have long ago stopped interacting and may be separated by such a vast distance that communication between them is no longer possible.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Space-Efficient Quantum Algorithm for Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithms with Resource Estimation
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article presents a significant optimization for quantum algorithms targeting elliptic curve cryptography, achieving a substantial 37% reduction in logical qubit requirements compared to previous implementations. The breakthrough lies in developing a more space-efficient approach to modular inversion, one of the most resource-intensive components of Shor's algorithm when applied to elliptic curves. For practical quantum cryptanalysis, this reduction from 2124 to 1333 logical qubits for 256-bit curves brings the hardware requirements meaningfully closer to what might be achievable with near-term fault-tolerant quantum computers. Given that elliptic curve cryptography secures much of today's internet traffic, these algorithmic improvements provide a clearer timeline for when quantum computers might pose a real threat to current encryption standards.

Solving the Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP) is critical for evaluating the quantum security of widely deployed elliptic-curve cryptosystems. Consequently, minimizing the number of logical qubits required to execute this algorithm is a key object. In implementations of Shor's algorithm, the space complexity is largely dictated by the modular inversion operation during point addition. Starting from the extended Euclidean algorithm (EEA), we refine the register-sharing method of Proos and Zalka and propose a space-efficient reversible modular inversion algorithm. We use length registers together with location-controlled arithmetic to store the intermediate variables in a compact form throughout the computation. We then optimize the stepwise update rules and give concrete circuit constructions for the resulting controlled arithmetic components. This leads to a modular inversion circuit that uses $3n + 4\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + O(1)$ logical qubits and $204n^2\log_2 n + O(n^2)$ Toffoli gates. By inserting this modular inversion component into the controlled affine point-addition circuit, we obtain a space-efficient algorithm for the ECDLP with $5n + 4\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + O(1)$ qubits and $O(n^3)$ Toffoli gates. In particular, for a 256-bit prime-field curve, our estimate reduces the logical-qubit count to 1333, compared with 2124 in the previous low-width implementation of Häner et al.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: QuiX Quantum Demonstrates Below-Threshold Error Mitigation in Photonic Hardware
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough marks a critical milestone for photonic quantum computing, as QuiX Quantum has achieved the elusive "below-threshold" error mitigation where the benefits of error correction outweigh the computational overhead it introduces. While superconducting and trapped-ion systems have dominated quantum error correction research, this demonstration proves that photonic platforms can also cross this essential threshold needed for practical quantum advantage. The collaboration with NASA's Quantum AI Lab adds significant credibility to the results and suggests photonic systems may offer unique advantages for certain quantum applications, particularly those requiring room-temperature operation and natural connectivity to optical communication networks. This achievement helps validate photonics as a viable pathway toward fault-tolerant quantum computing, diversifying the technological approaches racing toward practical quantum systems.

QuiX Quantum, in collaboration with NASA’s Quantum Artificial Intelligence Laboratory, the University of Twente, and Freie Universität Berlin, has demonstrated "below-threshold" error mitigation on a photonic quantum computer. The experiment, conducted on the QuiX Bia™ Cloud Quantum Computing Service, represents the first hardware-level verification of net-positive error reduction in a photonic system. By suppressing physical [...] The post QuiX Quantum Demonstrates Below-Threshold Error Mitigation in Photonic Hardware appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Twinning! Quantum ‘Digital Twins’ Tackle Error Correction Task to Speed Path to Reliable Quantum Computers
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights an intriguing approach where researchers are using classical computers to create "digital twins" of quantum systems, essentially detailed simulations that can help predict and optimize quantum error correction strategies before implementing them on actual quantum hardware. This classical-quantum hybrid methodology could significantly accelerate the development of fault-tolerant quantum computers by allowing researchers to test error correction protocols virtually, saving precious quantum computing time and resources that are currently extremely limited and expensive. The timing is particularly notable given the recent surge in quantum error correction breakthroughs, suggesting the field is converging on multiple complementary approaches to solve one of quantum computing's most fundamental challenges. By leveraging the computational power of classical systems to guide quantum system design, this work represents a pragmatic bridge between today's noisy quantum devices and tomorrow's error-corrected quantum computers.

Insider Brief In a week that has seen a parade of error-correction advances, a team of quantum computing researchers say they have shown a practical way to simulate large, error-prone quantum systems on classical computers, an advance that the scientists suggest could speed the path toward reliable quantum machines. A collaboration involving Amazon Web Services, […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Explicit constructions of mutually unbiased bases via Hadamard matrices
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of the most fundamental mathematical puzzles in quantum information theory: constructing complete sets of mutually unbiased bases (MUBs), which are essential building blocks for quantum cryptography, state tomography, and error correction protocols. The work provides explicit analytical methods for generating these special quantum measurement bases in small dimensions, with particular attention to the notoriously difficult dimension-6 case where a complete solution has eluded researchers for decades. By bridging abstract group theory with practical computational methods, this study offers quantum engineers concrete tools for designing optimal measurement schemes while advancing our theoretical understanding of quantum state space geometry. The systematic framework presented here could accelerate the development of more efficient quantum protocols by providing clearer pathways to construct the mathematical foundations needed for robust quantum information processing.

We present a detailed computational and algebraic study of Mutually Unbiased Bases (MUBs) in finite-dimensional Hilbert spaces, with a particular focus on dimensions 2, 3, 4, and the challenging case of 6. Starting from the Hadamard-phase parametrization, we derive explicit analytical conditions for mutual unbiasedness in dimension 4, providing a tractable system of trigonometric constraints on the phase parameters. We then explore a tensor-product construction via Pauli operators, highlighting the algebraic and group-theoretical origin of MUBs in two-qubit systems, and demonstrating how these constructions yield a complete set of 5 MUBs in dimension 4. Extending our approach, we investigate the Fourier-family method in dimension 6, where the absence of a prime-power structure imposes strong rigidity constraints and limits the known constructions to sets of 3 MUBs. We provide a systematic computational framework for testing candidate phase vectors, bridging the gap between analytical insight and numerical exploration. Finally, we generalize the discussion to arbitrary prime-power dimensions, emphasizing the role of finite-field structures, Heisenberg-Weyl operators, and discrete symmetries in generating complete sets of MUBs. Our work offers a transparent, line-by-line verification methodology, highlighting both the geometric and algebraic richness of MUBs, and clarifying why certain dimensions resist full analytical constructions. This study serves as a comprehensive resource for researchers seeking both theoretical understanding and practical construction of MUBs in quantum information science.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: The Phase Quantum Walk: A Unified Framework for Graph State Distribution in Quantum Networks
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article introduces a clever new approach to distributing complex quantum states across networks by replacing traditional quantum walk movements with entangling gates, solving a key bottleneck in building modular quantum computers where different processors need to share intricate quantum information. The breakthrough lies in proving that any graph state can be distributed with the same fidelity regardless of its topology - a surprising result that dramatically simplifies network design since engineers no longer need to optimize protocols for each specific quantum state structure. The experimental validation on IBM's hardware provides crucial real-world confirmation of the theory, demonstrating identical performance for different quantum states as predicted. This work could accelerate the development of distributed quantum computing architectures and quantum internet protocols by providing a unified, hardware-efficient method for sharing quantum information across network nodes.

Distributing arbitrary graph states across quantum networks is a central challenge for modular quantum computing and measurement-based quantum communication. I introduce the phase quantum walk (PQW), a discrete-time quantum walk in which the conventional position-permuting shift operator is replaced by a diagonal conditional phase (CZ) gate, enabling distribution of arbitrary graph states, not merely GHZ states, from elementary two-qubit resources. The Byproduct Lemma shows that each walk step teleports edge entanglement with a correctable Pauli byproduct; the Coin Invariance Theorem proves that the optimal fidelity F*(C,E) = F*(H,E) for all unitary coins C and noise channels E, with closed-form expressions F_dep = (1 - 3p/4)^k and F_pd = ((1 + sqrt(1 - p))/2)^k. Analytical correction formulas are derived for tree graphs (general theorem) and ring graphs (C4 case study), with F = 1.0 verified across eight topologies (up to 4096 outcomes). Hardware validation on ibm marrakesh (IBM Heron r2, CZ-native) yields F_cl = 0.924 for |GHZ4> and 0.922 for |L4>, statistically identical, providing the first experimental confirmation that fidelity is independent of graph topology as predicted by the Coin Invariance Theorem.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: QuEra Releases Open-Source GPU-Accelerated T-Gate Simulator for Error Correction Research
item.description:
Curator's Take: This release addresses a critical bottleneck in quantum error correction research by providing researchers with a powerful tool to simulate the non-Clifford T-gates that are essential for universal quantum computation but computationally expensive to model at scale. While existing simulators like STIM excel at Clifford operations, QuEra's GPU-accelerated Tsim fills the gap for studying how these crucial but "magic" gates behave within error correction protocols - a key piece of the puzzle for building fault-tolerant quantum computers. The open-source nature of this tool democratizes access to sophisticated QEC simulation capabilities, potentially accelerating research across the field as teams can now perform the large-scale statistical studies needed to optimize error correction schemes for practical quantum algorithms.

QuEra Computing has released Tsim, an open-source, GPU-accelerated quantum circuit simulator designed to model non-Clifford gate operations. The tool addresses a technical gap in the development of quantum error correction (QEC) protocols, which require high-speed, large-scale statistical analysis of universal quantum circuits. While established simulators such as STIM focus on Clifford gates, Tsim provides the [...] The post QuEra Releases Open-Source GPU-Accelerated T-Gate Simulator for Error Correction Research appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quanscient and Haiqu Demonstrate Algorithm for Scalable Computational Nonlinear Fluid Simulations
item.description:
Curator's Take: This demonstration marks a significant milestone in quantum computational fluid dynamics, as the team achieved the most complex hardware implementation of Quantum Lattice Boltzmann Method to date by successfully simulating nonlinear fluid flow around an obstacle. What makes this particularly noteworthy is that CFD simulations are computationally intensive workloads that could benefit enormously from quantum acceleration, potentially revolutionizing everything from aircraft design to weather modeling if the approach can scale effectively. The successful execution on IBM's Heron hardware suggests that near-term quantum devices may already be capable of handling meaningful fluid dynamics problems, though the real test will be demonstrating quantum advantage over classical methods as problem sizes increase. This work represents important progress toward practical quantum applications in engineering and scientific computing, moving beyond toy problems to simulations that mirror real-world complexity.

Quanscient and Haiqu have announced a new quantum algorithm designed to accelerate Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) simulations. The researchers successfully executed a 15-step nonlinear fluid benchmark involving an obstacle, which currently stands as the most physically complex hardware demonstration of a Quantum Lattice Boltzmann Method (QLBM). The benchmark was conducted on the IBM Heron R3 [...] The post Quanscient and Haiqu Demonstrate Algorithm for Scalable Computational Nonlinear Fluid Simulations appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Constrained Quantum Optimization via Iterative Warm-Start XY-Mixers
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of QAOA's most persistent headaches: how to efficiently handle hard constraints while still benefiting from warm-starting techniques that guide the algorithm toward promising solutions. The breakthrough here is developing a modified XY-mixer Hamiltonian that maintains mathematical rigor while allowing the algorithm to start from biased initial states, essentially letting QAOA have its cake and eat it too. The researchers demonstrate orders of magnitude improvement in finding optimal solutions for classic problems like Max-k-Cut and TSP, which could make QAOA far more practical for real-world constrained optimization problems that plague logistics, finance, and supply chain management. This work represents a significant step toward making near-term quantum algorithms genuinely competitive with classical methods for commercially relevant optimization challenges.

The Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (QAOA) is a leading hybrid heuristic for combinatorial optimization, but efficiently handling hard constraints remains a significant challenge. XY-mixers successfully confine quantum state evolution to a feasible subspace, such as the Hamming-weight-1 sector for one-hot constraints. On the contrary, warm-starting biases the search toward promising regions based on preliminary solutions. Combining these two techniques requires maintaining the essential alignment between the initial state and the mixer Hamiltonian to preserve convergence guarantees. Previous work demonstrated warm-starting with XY-mixers via a biased initial state, but relying only on standard mixer Hamiltonians. Consequently, the initial state is no longer a ground state of the mixer. In this work, we overcome these limitations by formulating a warm-started XY-mixer Hamiltonian for one-hot constraints and proving its ground-state properties. Furthermore, we provide a shallow circuit implementation suitable for NISQ implementations. We embed the warm-starting into a classical heuristic that iteratively updates the bias based on previous samples, called Iterative Warm-Starting (IWS). Extensive numerical simulations on Max-$k$-Cut and Traveling Salesperson Problem instances demonstrate that IWS-QAOA significantly accelerates the solution-finding process, increasing the probability of sampling optimal solutions by orders of magnitude compared to standard XY-QAOA. Finally, we validate our approach on the ibm_boston QPU using hardware-tailored 144-qubit problem instances. By coupling IWS-QAOA with a greedy steepest-descent post-processing strategy to repair infeasible measurements caused by hardware noise, we successfully identify optimal solutions on actual quantum devices.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Photonic qubit encoding interconversion for heterogeneous quantum networking
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles one of quantum networking's most pressing practical challenges: how to connect different quantum systems that "speak" different photonic languages. While some quantum devices encode information in photon polarization and others use time-bin encoding, this research demonstrates a clever interconversion protocol that acts like a universal translator, allowing polarization-encoded qubits to survive transmission through fiber optic cables by temporarily converting them to the more robust time-bin format. The team's approach of converting fidelity losses into transmission rate changes is particularly elegant, maintaining the quality of quantum entanglement even when the fiber introduces polarization fluctuations. This kind of encoding flexibility will be essential for building large-scale quantum networks where different institutions and companies need to connect their disparate quantum hardware platforms seamlessly.

Quantum information processing, communication, and sensing networks are being developed with various qubit platforms that use different encoding schemes. Connecting quantum network nodes to distribute entanglement requires matching photon qubit basis encoding. In this work, we implement an interconversion protocol which converts photon qubit encoding from the polarization basis to the time-bin basis, transmits the photons through a transport fiber with large fluctuations in polarization, and converts back to polarization encoding for ease of measurement. This interconversion scheme faithfully transmits a polarization Bell state across the transport fiber by converting sources of infidelity to changes in transmission rate. These results illustrate a practical approach for interfacing distinct qubit platforms to enable modular and flexible operation in heterogeneous quantum networks.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Emergence of volume-law scaling for entanglement negativity from the Hawking radiation of analogue black holes
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article presents a groundbreaking theoretical framework for understanding how Hawking radiation creates entanglement patterns that could be observable in laboratory experiments using cold atoms to simulate black hole physics. The researchers demonstrate that the usually divergent mathematical quantities describing quantum entanglement become finite and measurable when Hawking radiation is present, revealing how quantum information is distributed between the interior and exterior of a black hole. This work bridges the gap between abstract black hole physics and concrete experimental possibilities, offering a pathway to test fundamental questions about information loss and unitarity in black hole evaporation using tabletop quantum simulators. The findings could provide crucial insights into one of physics' most perplexing puzzles while advancing our understanding of how quantum entanglement behaves in curved spacetime.

The quantum information content of Hawking radiation holds the key to understanding black-hole evaporation and the fate of unitarity. Motivated by recent advances in cold-atom experiments, we develop a lattice-regularization approach aimed at simulating the coarse-grained entanglement scaling of a quantum field in a 1+1D analogue black-hole background. We provide the first concrete demonstration that logarithmic negativity -- an entanglement monotone that typically exhibits a UV-divergent log-scaling for the conformal vacuum -- acquires a UV-finite volume term from the nonlocal correlations seeded by Hawking radiation. We show that this volume term encodes both the number density and spatial distribution of entangled Hawking pairs along the black-hole interior and exterior. We highlight its prospective detection in currently realizable experiments as well as its implications beyond the analogue paradigm, in particular for black-hole thermodynamics.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Decoding Realistic Quantum Error Syndrome with Quantum Elements Digital Twins
item.description:
Curator's Take: This AWS research tackles one of the most critical practical challenges in quantum computing: determining the exact hardware quality and code size needed to achieve fault-tolerant quantum computation in real-world conditions. By using digital twins to simulate realistic error syndromes rather than idealized theoretical models, the work bridges the gap between laboratory quantum error correction demonstrations and the engineering requirements for practical quantum computers. This approach could significantly accelerate the timeline for fault-tolerant quantum systems by helping hardware developers understand precisely what error rates they need to target and helping software teams optimize error correction codes for actual device characteristics rather than theoretical assumptions.

Fault-tolerant quantum computing requires quantum error correction (QEC): Encoding one logical qubit into many physical qubits so that, below a threshold error rate, the logical error rate falls rapidly as the code grows. The practical engineering question is: How large must the code be and how good must the hardware be to reach a useful […]

Read original at AWS Quantum Computing Blog
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum search algorithm for similar subgraph identification under fixed edge removal
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article presents a quantum algorithm that tackles the computationally intensive problem of finding similar subgraphs by identifying which edges to remove from a reference graph to best match a target structure. The approach cleverly uses Dicke states to represent all possible graph configurations in quantum superposition, then applies amplitude estimation to achieve a polynomial speedup over classical brute-force methods. What makes this particularly intriguing is the practical application to power grid analysis, where understanding network resilience and identifying critical infrastructure components is crucial for maintaining electrical grid stability. While the quantum advantage is significant in theory, the real-world impact will depend on whether near-term quantum computers can handle the complex state preparations and controlled operations required for graphs of practical size.

We introduce a novel quantum algorithm for similar subgraph identification in form of an NP-hard cardinality-constrained binary quadratic optimization problem. Given a weighted reference graph with Laplacian $\boldsymbol{B}$, our algorithm determines the subgraph featuring Laplacian $\boldsymbol{B'}$ on the same vertex set, but $x$ out of $N$ inactive edges, minimizing the Frobenius distance $||\boldsymbol{B} - \boldsymbol{B'}||_\mathrm{F}^2$. We represent the $\binom{N}{x}$ graph topologies by an equal-weight superposition in form of a Dicke state, enabling controlled transformations applied to the quantum state associated with the vectorized Laplacian of the reference graph. Combined with amplitude estimation and a minimum finding approach, our algorithm provides a polynomial speed up $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{N^{x}/x!}N\log\log N)$ compared to $\mathcal{O}(N^{x+1}/x!)$ of classical brute-force search algorithms. We demonstrate the application of our method on standard test cases, which represent electric power grids, by reconstructing $||\boldsymbol{B} -\boldsymbol{B'}||_\mathrm{F}^2$ from measurements and show how our approach can be additionally used to calculate energy functional like quadratic forms of the Laplacians with respect to a given vector.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Dancing to Invisible Choreography, Quantum Computers Can Balance The Noise
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights one of quantum computing's most fundamental challenges: quantum noise, which causes the fragile quantum states that power these machines to degrade and produce errors. The "dancing to invisible choreography" metaphor cleverly captures how quantum systems must maintain perfect coherence despite constant environmental interference that threatens to destroy their delicate quantum properties. While the brief doesn't reveal specific technical breakthroughs, the focus on noise mitigation is crucial since current quantum computers can only perform limited calculations before errors accumulate beyond useful levels. Progress in quantum error correction and noise reduction techniques will ultimately determine when we transition from today's noisy intermediate-scale quantum devices to the fault-tolerant quantum computers that could revolutionize fields from cryptography to drug discovery.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Large-scale quantum computers are waiting in the wings. One of the main reasons we don’t have them yet is because quantum hardware is so noisy. This isn’t the type of noise you’d want to shush in a crowded theater. When it comes to computers, noise means errors that crop up […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Infleqtion Delivers Quantum Precision Timing for Mission-Critical Systems
item.description:
Curator's Take: This announcement marks a significant step toward real-world quantum sensing applications, as Infleqtion's Tiqker quantum optical clock technology moves from laboratory demonstrations to actual deployment in mission-critical systems through their partnership with defense contractor Safran. Quantum-enhanced precision timing represents one of the nearer-term practical applications of quantum technology, offering unprecedented accuracy that could revolutionize GPS-independent navigation, secure communications, and critical infrastructure timing. The collaboration between a quantum startup and an established defense manufacturer signals growing industry confidence in quantum sensing technologies and suggests we may see broader commercial adoption of quantum timing solutions in the coming years. This deployment could serve as a crucial proof-of-concept for quantum sensing in high-stakes environments where timing precision can literally be a matter of life and death.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Infleqtion (NYSE: INFQ), a global leader in quantum sensing and quantum computing powered by neutral-atom technology, announced availability of the first quantum-enabled precision timing solution delivered as part of the company’s partnership with global civil and military equipment manufacturer Safran Electronics & Defense. The new solution includes Infleqtion’s Tiqker™ quantum optical […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: QuiX Quantum Demonstrates Below-Threshold Error Mitigation in Photonic Quantum Computing For First Time
item.description:
Curator's Take: This milestone represents a crucial step toward practical photonic quantum computing, as achieving below-threshold error rates means the quantum error correction can actually reduce errors faster than they accumulate during computation. QuiX Quantum's demonstration is particularly significant because photonic systems have traditionally lagged behind superconducting and trapped-ion platforms in error correction capabilities, making this the first proof that light-based quantum computers can reach the quality thresholds needed for fault-tolerant operation. The breakthrough positions photonic quantum computing as a more viable contender for large-scale quantum applications, especially given photons' natural advantages for networking and room-temperature operation. This achievement also marks Europe's growing presence in the quantum hardware race, traditionally dominated by US and Chinese companies.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — QuiX Quantum, a leading provider of photonic quantum computing hardware, today announced it has demonstrated “below threshold” error mitigation for the first time on a photonic quantum computer, suppressing physical qubit errors to the level compatible with scalable, fault‑tolerant quantum computing. The achievement marks the first time a European company […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Towards Chemically Accurate and Scalable Quantum Simulations on IQM Quantum Hardware: A Quantum-HPC Hybrid Approach
item.description:
Curator's Take: This comprehensive study represents a significant milestone in demonstrating quantum computing's potential for practical molecular simulation, showcasing impressive scale by utilizing 16 qubits on IQM's hardware to tackle real chemical systems like water and pharmaceutical compounds. The researchers' hybrid quantum-classical approach, combining Sample-based Quantum Diagonalization with classical embedding techniques, offers a promising pathway to overcome current hardware limitations while still achieving chemically meaningful results. Particularly noteworthy is their construction of a full 2D potential energy surface for water directly on quantum hardware—a computationally intensive task that demonstrates how quantum processors might eventually revolutionize drug discovery and materials science. The work's extension to pharmacologically relevant molecules like amantadine suggests we're approaching a threshold where quantum advantage in chemistry could transition from theoretical possibility to practical reality.

We present a large-scale experimental study of quantum-computing-based molecular simulation carried out on IQM's Sirius 24-qubit superconducting processor, utilizing up to 16 operational qubits. The work employs Sample-based Quantum Diagonalization (SQD) together with the Local Unitary Cluster Jastrow (LUCJ) ansatz to estimate ground-state energies for a set of benchmark molecules, including H$_2$, LiH, BeH$_2$, H$_2$O, and NH$_3$. In addition, we introduce a Linear-CNOT variant of the Unitary Coupled-Cluster Singles and Doubles (LCNot-UCCSD) ansatz within the SQD workflow, trading higher circuit depth for reduced classical preprocessing. A comparison between these ansätze is provided, clarifying their respective strengths, limitations, and suitability for near-term quantum hardware. We further explore potential energy landscapes through 1D scans for H$_2$ and HeH$^+$ using both STO-3G and 6-31G basis sets, and for LiH and BeH$_2$ in STO-3G. Extending beyond this, we demonstrate the experimental construction of a full 2D potential energy surface for the water molecule on quantum hardware, mapped over a 32 $\times$ 32 grid in bond length and bond angle. To move beyond small benchmark systems, we combine SQD(LUCJ) with Density Matrix Embedding Theory (DMET) to compute active-space energies for a set of ligand-like molecules, as well as the pharmacologically relevant amantadine system. Across all studies, the majority of quantum-computed energies agree with reference FCI results, as well as with DMET-CASCI energies for embedded systems, to within chemical accuracy for the chosen basis sets. These results demonstrate the reliability of sample-based diagonalization approaches and underscore the potential of hybrid embedding strategies for extending quantum simulations to increasingly complex molecular systems, while also highlighting their practicality on current IQM quantum hardware.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quip.Network Launches Quantum-Classical Blockchain Testnet Built in Consultation with D-Wave
item.description:
Curator's Take: This announcement marks a significant step toward practical quantum-blockchain integration, as Quip.Network becomes the first to publicly deploy a quantum-classical hybrid blockchain testnet developed with guidance from D-Wave's quantum annealing expertise. The impressive 13,000+ participant signup demonstrates strong industry interest in exploring how quantum computing can enhance blockchain security and efficiency, particularly as classical cryptographic methods face future quantum threats. While still in testnet phase, this collaboration between Postquant Labs and D-Wave represents a tangible bridge between two transformative technologies, offering researchers real-world experience with quantum-enhanced distributed systems. The timing is particularly relevant as the blockchain industry increasingly seeks quantum-resistant solutions ahead of the anticipated arrival of cryptographically relevant quantum computers.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Postquant Labs, the developer building Quip.Network, today announced the launch of the first publicly available quantum-classical blockchain testnet, built in consultation with D-Wave Quantum Inc. (“D-Wave”), the world’s leading quantum computing company. More than 13,000 people have signed up to participate in the testnet, which enables the global research community […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Qubits Ventures Hosts Quantum Event in Pasadena
item.description:
Curator's Take: This networking event highlights the growing maturation of quantum computing as an investment sector, with dedicated venture capital firms like Qubits Ventures now actively cultivating the startup ecosystem. The choice of Caltech as the venue is particularly strategic, given the institution's leading role in quantum research and its proximity to both Silicon Valley tech culture and emerging quantum companies. While networking events themselves don't advance the technology, they serve as crucial catalysts for connecting researchers, entrepreneurs, and investors who can accelerate the translation of quantum breakthroughs from lab to market. The timing around World Quantum Day also reflects the field's increasing efforts to build public awareness and attract talent beyond traditional academic circles.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — As part of World Quantum Day and Innovate Pasadena’s Connect Week, Qubits Ventures, a venture capital firm investing in early-stage quantum and future of computing tech startups across the globe, is hosting Quantum Day Pasadena on April 14, 2026 at the California Institute of Technology (Cal Tech). Quantum Day aims to […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum Networking Fundamentals: From Physical Protocols to Network Engineering
item.description:
Curator's Take: This comprehensive tutorial tackles one of quantum networking's most critical challenges: the massive gap between laboratory quantum experiments and the engineering reality of building a scalable Quantum Internet. The authors make a compelling case that current quantum network development suffers from a "physics-first" approach that neglects the sophisticated orchestration and control systems needed for real-world deployment, much like early internet development required moving beyond simple point-to-point connections. Their proposed Software-Defined Quantum Networking framework could be transformative, providing the architectural foundation needed to manage fragile quantum resources across multi-user networks while maintaining the delicate balance between quantum fidelity and network performance. This work represents essential bridge-building between quantum physicists and network engineers, potentially accelerating the timeline for practical quantum networking applications from distributed quantum computing to ultra-secure communications.

The realization of the Quantum Internet promises transformative capabilities in secure communication, distributed quantum computing, and high-precision metrology. However, transitioning from laboratory experiments to a scalable, multi-tenant network utility introduces deep orchestration challenges. Current development is often siloed within physics communities, prioritizing hardware, while the classical networking community lacks architectural models to manage fragile quantum resources. This tutorial bridges this divide by providing a network-centric view of quantum networking. We dismantle idealized assumptions in current simulators to address the "simulation-reality gap," recasting them as explicit control-plane constraints. To bridge this gap, we establish Software-Defined Quantum Networking (SDQN) as a prerequisite for scale, prioritizing a symbiotic, dual-plane architecture where classical control dictates quantum data flow. Specifically, we synthesize reference models for SDQN and the Quantum Network Operating System (QNOS) for hardware abstraction, and adapt a Quantum Network Utility Maximization (Q-NUM) framework as a unifying mathematical lens for engineers to reason about trade-offs between entanglement routing, scheduling, and fidelity. Furthermore, we analyze Distributed Quantum AI (DQAI) over imperfect networks as a case study, illustrating how physical constraints such as probabilistic stragglers and decoherence dictate application-layer viability. Ultimately, this tutorial equips network engineers with the tools required to transition quantum networking from a bespoke physics experiment into a programmable, multi-tenant global infrastructure.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Researchers Demonstrate Stable Links For Quantum Networks Over Kilometers of Noisy Fiber
item.description:
Curator's Take: This demonstration represents a crucial milestone in the practical deployment of quantum networks, as maintaining quantum coherence over long distances through real-world fiber infrastructure has been one of the biggest engineering challenges facing the field. The ability to preserve quantum properties across kilometers of "noisy" fiber - the kind actually deployed in telecommunications networks rather than pristine laboratory conditions - brings us significantly closer to a functional quantum internet that could connect quantum computers and enable ultra-secure communications. This work addresses the gap between laboratory quantum networking experiments and the messy reality of existing fiber infrastructure, potentially accelerating the timeline for quantum network deployment by leveraging infrastructure that's already in the ground. The stabilization techniques developed here could become foundational for the first generation of metropolitan-scale quantum networks.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Researchers have shown that a single photon carrying quantum information can travel down kilometers of noisy real-world fiber and arrive at the far end with their quantum properties largely preserved. These highly stabilized fiber links represent a key step toward building a fast and reliable quantum network. “Scalable quantum networks […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Topology-Hiding Connectivity-Assurance for QKD Inter-Networking
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles a critical trust problem in quantum key distribution networks by developing a clever cryptographic solution that lets network operators prove secure connections exist without revealing sensitive network topology details. The work addresses a real vulnerability in QKD deployments where trusted repeaters create potential attack vectors, offering a zero-knowledge approach that could significantly enhance the security and privacy of quantum communication infrastructure. By enabling verification of multiple disjoint paths while keeping network architecture hidden, this protocol could prove essential for large-scale quantum internet deployments where network providers need to collaborate without exposing their internal infrastructure. The bridge between classical cryptographic techniques and quantum networking requirements represents an important step toward making quantum communication networks both more secure and more practical for real-world deployment.

While QKD ensures information-theoretic security at the link level, real-world deployments depend on trusted repeaters, creating potential vulnerabilities. In this paper, we thus introduce a topology-hiding connectivity assurance protocol to enhance trust in quantum key distribution (QKD) network infrastructures. Our protocol allows network providers to jointly prove the existence of a secure connection between endpoints without revealing internal topology details. By extending graph-signature techniques to support multi-graphs and hidden endpoints, we enable zero-knowledge proofs of connectivity that ensure both soundness and topology hiding. We further discuss how our approach can certify, e.g., multiple disjoint paths, supporting multi-path QKD scenarios. This work bridges cryptographic assurance methods with the operational requirements of QKD networks, promoting verifiable and privacy-preserving inter-network connectivity.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Topology-Hiding Path Validation for Large-Scale Quantum Key Distribution Networks
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles a crucial trust problem that will emerge as quantum key distribution networks scale beyond laboratory demonstrations into real-world deployments. While QKD promises theoretically unbreakable encryption, practical networks require trusted repeater nodes and network operators, creating potential vulnerabilities that could undermine the entire security promise. The proposed topology-hiding validation protocol offers an elegant solution by allowing users to verify their communications follow agreed-upon secure paths without revealing sensitive network infrastructure details to potential adversaries. With computational costs under 2.5 seconds for 100-node networks, this work provides a practical foundation for the trust infrastructure that large-scale quantum communication networks will need to gain widespread adoption.

Secure long-distance communication in quantum key distribution (QKD) networks depends on trusted repeater nodes along the entire transmission path. Consequently, these nodes will be subject to strict auditing and certification in future large-scale QKD deployments. However, trust must also extend to the network operator, who is responsible for fulfilling contractual obligations -- such as ensuring certified devices are used and transmission paths remain disjoint where required. In this work, we present a path validation protocol specifically designed for QKD networks. It enables the receiver to verify compliance with agreed-upon policies. At the same time, the protocol preserves the operator's confidentiality by ensuring that no sensitive information about the network topology is revealed to users. We provide a formal model and a provably secure generic construction of the protocol, along with a concrete instantiation. For long-distance communication involving 100 nodes, the protocol has a computational cost of 1-2.5s depending on the machine, and a communication overhead of less than 70kB - demonstrating the efficiency of our approach.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Novel approach to quantum error correction portends a scalable future for quantum computing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough addresses one of quantum computing's most fundamental scalability challenges by potentially slashing the enormous overhead typically required for quantum error correction. Traditional fault-tolerant quantum computing schemes often demand hundreds or thousands of physical qubits to protect each logical qubit, making large-scale quantum computers prohibitively expensive and complex. Dr. Williamson's novel "gauging" approach to logical operators could dramatically reduce this overhead, bringing practical, error-corrected quantum computers closer to reality. This development is particularly significant because it tackles the resource requirements from a theoretical angle, potentially making quantum advantage achievable with far fewer physical qubits than previously thought necessary.

A University of Sydney quantum physicist has developed a new approach to quantum error correction that could significantly reduce the number of physical qubits required to build large-scale, fault-tolerant quantum computers. The study, co-authored by Dr. Dominic Williamson from the School of Physics, is titled "Low-overhead fault-tolerant quantum computation by gauging logical operators" and published in Nature Physics.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Distribution of Bell State Entanglement in Qubit Networks via Collision Models
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article presents a clever approach to one of quantum networking's fundamental challenges: how to create entanglement between qubits that aren't directly connected to each other. The researchers demonstrate that by using "collision models" - where ancilla qubits sequentially interact with network nodes and then move on - they can generate Bell state entanglement even between distant qubits, essentially using the ancillas as quantum messengers. What makes this particularly promising is that the scheme works even when the ancilla only touches one node at a time, which could dramatically simplify the engineering requirements for building larger quantum networks. This collision-based approach offers a practical pathway toward scalable quantum communication networks and could be especially valuable for connecting separate quantum processing modules in future quantum computers.

We propose a general scheme to controllably distribute pairwise entanglement in a quantum network of qubits by exploiting environmental ancilla qubits interacting with the network nodes through tunable Hamiltonians. Our approach leverages collision models, in which a quantum syatem interacts sequentially with ancilla units. We explore two distinct scenarios within this framework: one in which the ancilla is reset to its initial coherent state after each interaction (the traditional collision model), and another where the ancilla is not reset but its state is simply carried over to the next interaction, which we dub the repeated interaction model. In both scenarios, we ensure that the system-ancilla correlations are discarded between steps. We also demonstrate how varying the ancilla-system interaction patterns enables selective generation of entanglement between different qubit pairs, including non-neighbouring nodes that do not directly interact. The scheme is analyzed in networks up to three qubits under both collision and repeated interaction dynamics, revealing the genaration of maximally entangled bell pairs even in configurations where the interacting ancilla couples to only a single node. Our results provide a systematic and physically implementable route to entanglement distribution, offering potential applications in quantum communication, metrology and modular quantum computing.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Trapped ion quantum technology gets smaller
item.description:
Curator's Take: This development represents a crucial step toward practical quantum technologies, as trapped ion systems have traditionally required large, room-sized setups with complex laser and vacuum systems. Making these systems portable could democratize access to quantum computing resources and enable new applications like mobile quantum sensors or field-deployable atomic clocks. The miniaturization also suggests that trapped ion quantum computers might eventually compete with other platforms not just on performance, but on practical deployment considerations. While the article summary is brief, portable trapped ion devices could bridge the gap between laboratory demonstrations and real-world quantum applications across multiple domains.

Portable device could find applications in quantum computing and optical clocks The post Trapped ion quantum technology gets smaller appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum-Enhanced Processing with Tensor-Network Frontends for Privacy-Aware Federated Medical Diagnosis
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of the most pressing challenges in quantum computing applications: bridging the gap between current hardware limitations and real-world problems that demand both computational power and privacy protection. By cleverly using tensor networks to compress medical images into manageable representations before quantum processing, the authors demonstrate a practical path toward quantum-enhanced federated learning that could actually run on today's noisy intermediate-scale quantum devices. The privacy-preserving aspect is particularly compelling for healthcare applications, where sensitive medical data cannot be shared directly between institutions, yet collaborative AI models could dramatically improve diagnostic accuracy. While the quantum enhancement appears modest in current experiments, this hybrid classical-quantum approach represents exactly the kind of pragmatic framework needed to demonstrate quantum advantage in real-world scenarios before fault-tolerant quantum computers arrive.

We propose a privacy-aware hybrid framework for federated medical image classification that combines tensor-network representation learning, MPC-secured aggregation, and post-aggregation quantum refinement. The framework is motivated by two practical constraints in privacy-aware federated learning: MPC can introduce substantial communication overhead, and direct quantum processing of high-dimensional medical images is unrealistic with a small number of qubits. To address both constraints within a single architecture, client-side tensor-network frontends, Matrix Product State (MPS), Tree Tensor Network (TTN), and Multi-scale Entanglement Renormalization Ansatz (MERA), compress local inputs into compact latent representations, after which a Quantum-Enhanced Processor (QEP) refines the aggregated latent feature through quantum-state embedding and observable-based readout. Experiments on PneumoniaMNIST show that the effect of the QEP is frontend-dependent rather than uniform across architectures. In the present setting, the TTN+QEP combination exhibits the most balanced overall profile. The results also suggest that the QEP behaves more stably when the qubit count is sufficiently matched to the latent dimension, while noisy conditions degrade performance relative to the noiseless setting. The MPC benchmark further shows that communication cost is governed primarily by the dimension of the protected latent representation. This indicates that tensor-network compression plays a dual role: it enables small-qubit quantum processing on compressed latent features and reduces the communication overhead associated with secure aggregation. Taken together, these results support a co-design perspective in which representation compression, post-aggregation quantum refinement, and privacy-aware deployment should be optimized jointly.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Distributed Variational Quantum Linear Solver
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of quantum computing's most pressing practical challenges: how to solve problems too large for any single quantum device by distributing the workload across multiple NISQ computers. The approach cleverly partitions large linear equation systems into smaller blocks that individual quantum computers can handle, then coordinates their efforts through distributed optimization - essentially creating a quantum computing cluster. While linear equation solving might sound academic, these systems are fundamental to everything from machine learning to engineering simulations, making this a potentially transformative step toward practical quantum advantage. The work demonstrates that quantum computing's future may lie not just in building bigger individual machines, but in orchestrating networks of smaller quantum devices to tackle real-world problems.

This paper develops a distributed variational quantum algorithm for solving large-scale linear equations. For a linear system of the form $Ax=b$, the large square matrix $A$ is partitioned into smaller square block submatrices, each of which is known only to a single noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) computer. Each NISQ computer communicates with certain other quantum computers in the same row and column of the block partition, where the communication patterns are described by the row- and column-neighbor graphs, both of which are connected. The proposed algorithm integrates a variant of the variational quantum linear solver at each computer with distributed classical optimization techniques. The derivation of the quantum cost function provides insight into the design of the distributed algorithm. Numerical quantum simulations demonstrate that the proposed distributed quantum algorithm can solve linear systems whose size scales with the number of computers and is therefore not limited by the capacity of a single quantum computer.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Useful quantum computers could be built with as few as 10,000 qubits, team finds
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough dramatically accelerates the timeline for practical quantum computing by slashing the qubit requirements by orders of magnitude. While previous estimates suggested millions of qubits would be needed for fault-tolerant quantum computers, this Caltech research demonstrates that sophisticated error correction techniques could enable useful quantum machines with just 10,000-20,000 qubits—a target that leading companies like IBM and Google might realistically achieve within this decade. The collaboration between theorists and experimentalists, including the Caltech spinoff Oratomic, highlights how academic research is rapidly translating into commercially viable approaches that could bring quantum advantage to real-world problems much sooner than anticipated.

Quantum computers of the future may be closer to reality thanks to new research from Caltech and Oratomic, a Caltech-linked start-up company. Theorists and experimentalists teamed up to develop a new approach for reducing the errors that riddle today's rudimentary quantum computers. Whereas these machines were previously thought to require millions of qubits to work properly (qubits being the quantum equivalent to 1's and 0's in classical computers), the new results indicate that a fully realized quantum computer could be built with as few as 10,000 to 20,000 qubits. The need for fewer qubits means that quantum computers could, in theory, be operational by the end of the decade.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: EPB Joins Southeastern Quantum Collaborative to Support Regional Infrastructure Integration
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights a crucial but often overlooked aspect of quantum computing development: the need for robust regional ecosystems that bridge academia, industry, and infrastructure providers. EPB's participation brings valuable telecommunications and energy expertise to the Southeastern Quantum Collaborative, which could prove essential as quantum networks and quantum data centers require specialized infrastructure support that traditional tech companies may lack. The collaboration between established quantum players like IBM, D-Wave, and IonQ with regional infrastructure specialists represents a maturing approach to quantum development that prioritizes practical deployment considerations alongside pure research. This type of regional coordination could serve as a model for other areas looking to build comprehensive quantum ecosystems rather than isolated research initiatives.

EPB has joined the Southeastern Quantum Collaborative (SQC) as an inaugural member. Led by the University of Alabama in Huntsville (UAH), the SQC is a regional association of academic, industrial, and government organizations—including IBM, D-Wave, and IonQ—tasked with coordinating quantum information science research and workforce development across the Southeast. EPB’s role in the collaborative involves [...] The post EPB Joins Southeastern Quantum Collaborative to Support Regional Infrastructure Integration appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: QuSecure Joins NIST NCCoE Consortium for Post-Quantum Cryptography Migration
item.description:
Curator's Take: This consortium represents a critical step in preparing for Y2Q - the "Years to Quantum" countdown when quantum computers will break current encryption standards. QuSecure's participation in NIST's NCCoE project signals that post-quantum cryptography migration is shifting from theoretical planning to practical implementation, as organizations need concrete roadmaps for replacing vulnerable legacy systems. The collaboration highlights the urgent need for industry-wide coordination in deploying quantum-resistant algorithms, since waiting until quantum computers actually threaten current encryption would leave organizations dangerously exposed. This kind of proactive consortium work is essential for ensuring a smooth transition to quantum-safe security before cryptographically relevant quantum computers emerge.

QuSecure has announced its participation in the Migration to Post-Quantum Cryptography Project consortium, organized by the National Cybersecurity Center of Excellence (NCCoE). This collaboration is designed to assist organizations in transitioning from current public-key algorithms to NIST-standardized quantum-resistant alternatives. The project addresses the technical and operational challenges associated with replacing legacy cryptography that is vulnerable [...] The post QuSecure Joins NIST NCCoE Consortium for Post-Quantum Cryptography Migration appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Silicon quantum computer performs logical operations for the first time
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough represents a crucial milestone for quantum computing's commercial viability, as silicon-based quantum processors could leverage the massive existing semiconductor manufacturing infrastructure rather than requiring entirely new fabrication methods. The demonstration of logical operations - where quantum information is protected through error correction rather than relying on fragile physical qubits - marks a significant step toward fault-tolerant quantum computing that can actually solve real-world problems. What makes this particularly exciting is that silicon spin qubits offer the tantalizing possibility of eventually integrating quantum processors directly alongside classical computer chips, potentially creating hybrid quantum-classical systems on a single piece of silicon. This convergence of quantum computing with the world's most mature semiconductor technology could dramatically accelerate the timeline for practical quantum applications.

Silicon is ubiquitous in modern electronics, and now it is becoming increasingly useful in quantum computing. In particular, silicon's compatibility with existing chip technology and its long coherence times in silicon-based spin qubits make it a promising material for scalable quantum computing. A new study, published in Nature Nanotechnology, has demonstrated silicon's use in a logical quantum processor, representing the first of its kind.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum computer accurately simulates real magnetic materials, reproducing national laboratory data
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article represents a crucial milestone in quantum computing's journey toward practical utility, demonstrating that today's quantum computers can accurately reproduce experimental data from national laboratories for real magnetic materials. This achievement validates quantum simulation as a viable complement to traditional computational methods, potentially accelerating materials discovery for applications ranging from energy storage to quantum technologies. The ability to simulate quantum materials on quantum hardware addresses one of classical computing's fundamental limitations, where the exponential complexity of quantum interactions makes accurate modeling prohibitively expensive. This success suggests we're approaching the point where quantum computers can provide genuine scientific insights beyond what classical methods can achieve, marking a significant step toward quantum advantage in materials science.

Studying and designing novel materials is a central application of quantum mechanics. Chemists, materials scientists, and physicists focus on subtle interactions in quantum materials and to uncover them they rely on sophisticated computational and experimental techniques. Computer simulations that connect microscopic quantum interactions to measurable material properties complement experimental data to connect structure to function—but classical computers can struggle to simulate those properties. Fortunately, scientists today have a new tool in their toolbox: quantum computers.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Unlocking scalable entanglement will enable next-generation quantum computing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights one of quantum computing's most fundamental challenges: creating and maintaining entanglement at scale, which is essential for quantum computers to deliver their promised exponential speedups over classical systems. While the summary touches on quantum computing's transformative potential, the real breakthrough will come when researchers can reliably generate entangled states across hundreds or thousands of qubits while preserving their delicate quantum properties from decoherence. Current quantum computers are limited by their ability to maintain entanglement across relatively small numbers of qubits, making scalable entanglement the key bottleneck that separates today's noisy intermediate-scale quantum devices from the fault-tolerant quantum computers needed for practical applications. Success in this area would represent a crucial step toward realizing quantum computing's full potential in drug discovery, materials science, and cryptography.

Quantum computing promises to transform our world in rapid, radical and revolutionary ways: solving in seconds problems that would take classical computers years, accelerating the discovery of new medicines, creating sustainable materials, optimizing complex systems, and strengthening cybersecurity. It does so using qubits, the quantum counterparts of classical bits, which can occupy multiple states simultaneously and enable a fundamentally new kind of computation.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Dual-rail superconducting qubits generate high-fidelity logical entanglement, study finds
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research represents a significant step toward fault-tolerant quantum computing by demonstrating that dual-rail superconducting qubits can achieve the high-fidelity logical entanglement necessary for quantum error correction. Unlike physical qubits that are prone to errors, these logical qubits encode quantum information across multiple physical qubits in a way that makes the system more resilient to noise and decoherence. The achievement of high-fidelity logical entanglement is crucial because it's the foundation for building larger, more reliable quantum computers that can perform complex calculations without being derailed by inevitable hardware imperfections. This work brings us closer to the holy grail of quantum computing: systems that can maintain quantum coherence long enough to solve real-world problems that are intractable for classical computers.

Quantum computers, systems that process information leveraging quantum mechanical effects, could outperform classical computers on some advanced tasks. These systems rely on qubits, the fundamental units of quantum information, that become linked via an effect known as quantum entanglement and share a unified quantum state.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Google Quantum AI Adopts Dual-Modality Strategy with Neutral Atom Expansion
item.description:
Curator's Take: Google's decision to pursue both superconducting and neutral atom quantum computing platforms represents a significant strategic pivot that acknowledges the fundamental uncertainties still facing the field. This dual-modality approach hedges against the possibility that superconducting qubits alone may not achieve the scale and coherence needed for practical quantum advantage, while neutral atoms offer compelling advantages in connectivity and potential for larger qubit counts. The move follows similar multi-platform strategies from other major players like Amazon and IBM, suggesting the industry is maturing beyond single-technology bets toward more diversified research portfolios. This expansion could accelerate progress by allowing Google to leverage the unique strengths of each platform while cross-pollinating insights between their hardware teams.

Google Quantum AI has announced a major strategic expansion of its research roadmap, adding neutral atom quantum computing to its established superconducting hardware program. Led by founder Hartmut Neven, the move signals a shift toward a multi-platform approach to reach commercially relevant quantum advantage. While Google remains confident in the timeline for superconducting systems by [...] The post Google Quantum AI Adopts Dual-Modality Strategy with Neutral Atom Expansion appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: New Zealand Researchers Report Advance in Optical Ising Machine for Hybrid Quantum Optimization
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research highlights an important trend in quantum computing where hybrid classical-quantum systems are emerging as practical stepping stones toward full-scale quantum advantage. Optical Ising machines represent a particularly promising approach because they can leverage quantum-inspired physics to tackle complex optimization problems without requiring the extreme engineering challenges of fault-tolerant quantum computers. The New Zealand team's focus on scalability is especially significant, as many current quantum and quantum-inspired systems struggle to maintain performance advantages as problem sizes grow. While we await the arrival of fully fault-tolerant quantum computers, these hybrid optimization devices could deliver real-world value in logistics, finance, and materials science much sooner than traditional quantum computing timelines suggest.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — With the world’s first scalable, fault-tolerant quantum computer expected to be years away, a group of New Zealand researchers has developed an exciting hybrid device that could solve big, intractable problems sooner. Research Fellow, Dr Liam Quinn of Te Whai Ao — Dodd-Walls Centre is leading the work, considered of […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Washington Begins to Treat Quantum Technology as a Strategic Priority
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article signals a crucial shift as quantum technology moves from research curiosity to national security imperative in Washington's policy circles. The bipartisan engagement suggests lawmakers are beginning to understand quantum's dual nature as both an enormous economic opportunity and a potential threat to current cryptographic systems that underpin everything from financial transactions to government communications. What makes this particularly significant is the timing - as quantum computers edge closer to practical applications, the window for establishing regulatory frameworks, funding priorities, and international cooperation agreements is narrowing rapidly. The fact that these discussions are happening now, rather than after quantum advantage becomes widespread, suggests policymakers may finally be getting ahead of the technology curve for once.

Insider Brief Tuesday in Washington offered a clear picture of where quantum technology now stands, not as a future concept, but as a present responsibility. Meetings on Capitol Hill, organized by Optica, brought together lawmakers, federal agencies, and leaders from across the quantum ecosystem to focus on what comes next. The conversations were not about […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Google Paves a Two-Lane Quantum Roadmap by Adding Neutral Atom Systems
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article signals a major strategic shift for Google Quantum AI, which has built its quantum supremacy claims and current leadership position around superconducting qubits but is now hedging its bets by exploring neutral atom systems. The move reflects growing industry recognition that different quantum hardware approaches may excel at different types of problems, with neutral atoms potentially offering advantages in connectivity and scalability that could complement Google's superconducting expertise. By diversifying its quantum hardware portfolio, Google is positioning itself to capitalize on whichever technology proves most viable for near-term commercial applications, while also acknowledging that the path to fault-tolerant quantum computing may require multiple parallel approaches rather than betting everything on a single platform.

Insider Brief Google Quantum AI, which was a pioneer in the superconducting approach to quantum computing, announced that it is expanding beyond that core approach to add neutral atom quantum computing, a move the company says will accelerate progress toward commercially useful machines. The plan, discussed in a recent Google Quantum AI blog post, would […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Scalable quantum circuit generation for iterative ground state approximation using Majorana Propagation
item.description:
Curator's Take: This work tackles one of quantum computing's most promising near-term applications - finding ground states of molecular systems - by cleverly combining classical preprocessing with quantum circuit design. The researchers demonstrate their ADAPT-VMPE algorithm can generate practical quantum circuits for systems as large as 100 qubits while maintaining polynomial scaling, which is crucial for making quantum chemistry simulations feasible on near-term devices. What makes this particularly exciting is their focus on a real-world photosensitizer molecule used in cancer treatment, showing how quantum algorithms could eventually accelerate drug discovery and materials science. The theoretical guarantees on approximation error and the polynomial complexity bounds suggest this approach could bridge the gap between today's noisy intermediate-scale quantum devices and useful quantum advantage in molecular simulation.

We introduce the Adaptive Derivative-Assembled Pseudo-Trotter ansatz Variational Majorana Propagation Eigensolver (ADAPT-VMPE), a quantum-inspired classical algorithm that exploits Majorana Propagation (MP) to produce circuits for approximating the ground state of molecular Hamiltonians. Equipped with the theoretical guarantees of MP, which provide controllable bounds on the approximation error, ADAPT-VMPE offers an efficient and scalable approach for iterative ansatz construction. A theoretical analysis of the computational complexity demonstrates that it is polynomial in both the number of qubits and the number of iterations. We present an in-depth analysis of circuit construction strategies, analyzing their impact on convergence and provide practical guidance for efficient ansatz generation. Using ADAPT-VMPE, we construct up to 100-qubit ansätze for a strongly correlated photosensitizer currently undergoing human clinical trials for cancer treatment. Our results demonstrate that constant overlap with the ground state across system sizes can be reached in polynomial time with polynomially sized circuits.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum simulation of Motzkin spin chain with Rydberg atoms
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article presents a breakthrough pathway for experimentally realizing Motzkin spin chains, exotic quantum systems that have tantalized theorists for years due to their unusual entanglement properties that violate fundamental scaling laws.
